title: Satellite-Based Quantum Communication: Performance Evaluation of Discrete-Variable Quantum Key Distribution Protocols
abstract: Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) has emerged as a fundamentally secure approach to communication in the era of quantum computing, offering protection against threats posed to classical cryptographic schemes such as RSA and Diffie-Hellman. This thesis presents a comprehensive performance analysis of satellite-based QKD protocols, focusing on both prepare-and-measure and entanglement-based schemes under realistic atmospheric and operational conditions. The study begins by introducing the theoretical foundations of quantum communication, including qubits, entanglement, and quantum entropy, and motivates the need for satellite-based QKD to overcome the distance limitations of fiber-based systems. Subsequently, the thesis evaluates four prominent QKD protocols-BB84, B92, BBM92, and E91-using a circular beam propagation model that incorporates atmospheric effects such as diffraction, turbulence, attenuation, and pointing errors, along with environmental noise contributions for uplink and downlink. Comparative numerical simulations reveal that protocol performance is strongly influenced by channel asymmetries, beam propagation characteristics, and noise, providing guidance on optimal protocol selection for low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite links. The research further investigates high-dimensional (HD) QKD protocols, specifically HD-BB84 and HD-Extended B92, using the elliptic-beam approximation to account for turbulence-induced distortions for both uplink and downlink. Simulations under vary ing system dimensions, weather conditions, and zenith angles demonstrate that HD-BB84 achieves higher key rates, superior noise tolerance, and more favorable probability distributions of the key rate compared to HD-Extended B92, highlighting the advantages of high-dimensional encoding for robust satellite-based QKD.
summary: This paper analyzes the performance of various Quantum Key Distribution protocols for satellite-based quantum links, accounting for realistic atmospheric distortions. It compares standard QKD methods against high-dimensional extensions, concluding that higher dimensions offer superior robustness in noisy space channels. The findings guide protocol selection for future LEO quantum communication networks.
main_problem: The primary goal is to perform a comprehensive performance evaluation of Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) protocols specifically designed for satellite-based quantum communication links.
main_result: High-Dimensional QKD protocol HD-BB84 was shown to achieve higher key rates and superior noise tolerance compared to HD-Extended B92 under realistic atmospheric conditions.
method: The analysis employs detailed numerical simulations using a circular/elliptic beam propagation model that incorporates complex atmospheric effects like diffraction, turbulence, attenuation, and pointing errors.
model_or_system: The system modeled is the free-space quantum channel between Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites and ground stations. Protocols evaluated include BB84, B92, BBM92, E91, and their high-dimensional generalizations.
key_observables: Key rate performance, Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), Probability Distribution of Key Rate (PDR), and conditional von Neumann entropy $S(a|E)$.
important_parameters: Atmospheric parameters ($C_n^2$), zenith angle ($\phi$), link length ($L$), and the depolarizing channel parameter ($q$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations such as the elliptic-beam approximation for turbulence effects and models environmental noise contributions (e.g., using a depolarizing channel).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate key rate and QBER variations as functions of zenith angle, link length, and under different weather conditions for various protocols.
paper_structure: The paper builds from theoretical foundations (qubits, entanglement) to protocol evaluation. It systematically compares standard vs. high-dimensional protocols across varying atmospheric/operational regimes, culminating in detailed rate derivations using entropy bounds.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the practical implementation challenges of quantum communication over long distances by rigorously modeling atmospheric channel impairments and comparing encoding schemes (2D vs HD).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2.6.1: (a) QBER and key rate versus zenith angle for the BB84 protocol in the uplink (night-time). (b) and (c) QBER and key rate versus zenith angle for the BB84 protocol in the downlink (day-time) and downlink (night-time), respectively.

Figure 2.6.2: (a) QBER and key rate versus zenith angle for the B92 protocol in the uplink (night- time). (b) and (c) QBER and key rate versus zenith angle for the B92 protocol in the downlink (day-time) and downlink (night-time), respectively.

Figure 2.6.3: (a) QBER and key rate versus zenith angle for the E91 protocol in the uplink (night- time). (b) and (c) QBER and key rate versus zenith angle for the E91 protocol in the downlink (day-time) and downlink (night-time), respectively.

Figure 2.6.4: (a) QBER and key rate versus zenith angle for the BBM92 protocol in the uplink (night-time). (b) and (c) QBER and key rate versus zenith angle for the BBM92 protocol in the downlink (day-time) and downlink (night-time), respectively.
Figure 2.6.5: QBER and key rate versus zenith angle for all considered QKD protocols: (a,d) uplink (night), (b,e) downlink (day), and (c,f) downlink (night).
